title: Hierarchical Particle Optimization for Cortical Shape Correspondence in Temporal Lobe Resection
abstract: Background:Anterior temporal lobe resection is an effective treatment for temporal lobe epilepsy. The post-surgical structural changes could influence the follow-up treatment. Capturing post-surgical changes necessitates a well-established cortical shape correspondence between pre- and post-surgical surfaces. Yet, most cortical surface registration methods are designed for normal neuroanatomy. Surgical changes can introduce wide ranging artifacts in correspondence, for which conventional surface registration methods may not work as intended.Methods:In this paper, we propose a novel particle method for one-to-one dense shape correspondence between pre- and post-surgical surfaces with temporal lobe resection. The proposed method can handle partial structural abnormality involving non-rigid changes. Unlike existing particle methods using implicit particle adjacency, we consider explicit particle adjacency to establish a smooth correspondence. Moreover, we propose hierarchical optimization of particles rather than full optimization of all particles at once to avoid trappings of locally optimal particle update. To the best of our knowledge, this is a relatively new work which propose a particle method from scratch with neighborhood information for correspondence problem in surgery.Results:We evaluate the proposed method on 25 pairs of T1-MRI with pre- and post-simulated resection on the anterior temporal lobe and 25 pairs of patients with actual resection. We show improved accuracy over several cortical regions in terms of ROI boundary Hausdorff distance with 4.29mm and Dice similarity coefficients with average value 0.841, compared to existing surface registration methods on simulated data. In 25 patients with actual resection of the anterior temporal lobe, our method shows an improved shape correspondence in qualitative and quantitative evaluation on parcellation-off ratio with average value 0.061 and cortical thickness changes. We also show better smoothness of the correspondence without self-intersection, compared with point-wise matching methods which show various degrees of self-intersection.Conclusion:The proposed method establishes a promising one-to-one dense shape correspondence for temporal lobe resection. The correspondence is smooth without self-intersection. The proposed hierarchical optimization strategy could accelerate optimization and improve the optimization accuracy. According to the results on paired surfaces with temporal lobe resection, this method outperforms the compared methods and is more reliable to capture cortical thickness changes.
discussion: 4.DiscussionStructural absence after resection yields partial distortion of human brain structure, which brings challenges to shape correspondence establishment. In this work, we proposed a particle method to address a dense cortical shape correspondence problem between pre- and post-surgical surfaces with temporal lobe resection. The main contribution of this work is that we proposed a particle method employing explicit neighborhood information and using hierarchical optimization. Specifically, we proposed a hierarchical optimization strategy to improve and accelerate optimization over a large set of particles by reducing trappings in local minima and address the self-intersection issue with a smooth shape correspondence. Note that without neighborhood information, hierarchical strategy could not be achieved in a classic particle method. As a result, our method yields an improved shape correspondence of cortical surfaces in temporal lobe resection.4.1Resected Region ClassifierAs discussed in Eq. 4, we used a sigmoid function r to penalize the particles located in resected regions in the energy terms. To control the behavior of r, we introduced t that regularizes the particle distances between pre- and post-surgical surfaces. The number of false positives may increase as t becomes smaller, while false negatives decrease as t becomes larger. The resected regions vary across 25 subjects and t is sensitive to the resection area. Unfortunately, the exact resected regions are unknown and need to be estimated. We instead excluded temporal lobe labels and examined false positives in the non-temporal lobe regions across the 25 subjects in Fig. 13, and almost no false positives are observed after t = 7 (0.3% out of a total non-resected surface area in the worst case). Therefore, we set t to 7 mm to estimate the resected region for all subjects in this work. Exploring a machine learning strategy to set this value according to different resections would be an interesting topic in the future work.4.2Optimization ParametersAnother important parameter is the step size α in Eq. 13. Although finding optimal step size is challenging, adaptive adjustment of step size has shown its effectiveness for fast convergence. Accordingly, we initially set α to 0.5 and adaptively adjust it during the optimization rather than keeping it constant. We scaled down α by a factor of 10, once the energy becomes increasing at one iteration. To the best of our knowledge, there is no systematical way to adjust α yet in present studies, so tuning of this hyperparameter still remains challenging. In the experiments, we found that the convergence speed was slow at a factor of 15, whereas the local minimum was found at a factor of 5. Despite heuristic tuning, we found that the optimization was well-balanced between computational speed and stability in its convergence at a factor of 10 in this work.The number of iterations is also an important parameter in our optimization. The proposed hierarchical optimization offers the well-aligned locations of particles in earlier levels, which reduces the number of iterations in the next levels by providing the reasonable initial guess (see Fig. 5). With hierarchical optimization, we found the energy converges up to 26 iterations at each level, while direct optimization over a full set of particles without hierarchy generally needs more than hundreds of iterations. According to the number of iterations, the proposed method would not increase the computation cost, which suggests that our method could be applicable for more fine-grained registration with more particles. More importantly, the optimization without the hierarchy is more vulnerable to local minima (see Fig. 5) due to the large number of particles to be optimized once without placement of known landmarks or aggressive surface inflation . Although we did not focus on exploring the parameter settings deeply in this work, it could be an interesting topic in the future work.4.3One-to-one smooth correspondenceOften, optimizing a full set of particles together yields a subset of particles being trapped in local minima due to the large number of particles to be optimized (see Fig. 5(b)). This also likely results in self-intersection in a surface mapping. To address such an optimization issue, we initialized particle locations in a hierarchical manner that consequently reduces self-intersection at each level. In turn, the proportion of self-intersection was noticeably reduced from 17% to 1.0% by the proposed hierarchical initialization. Note that our current approach cannot guarantee prevention of self-intersection in an analytical way. Instead, like other surface registration methods , we alternatively unfold flipped triangles at each hierarchical level of optimization, which could completely avoid the self-intersection. The unfolding procedure takes advantage of the particle neighborhood information. Meanwhile, the point-wise matching methods have relatively large self-intersection 9.4% (maximum=15.3%, stdev=2.59%), 3.2% (maximum=7.7%, stdev=1.77%), 17.6% (maximum=23.9%, stdev=3.12%) in ICP, CPD and SW, respectively. In these methods, unfolding triangles is non-trivial due to the absence of neighborhood information (see Fig. 10).4.4Cortical Features for Surface AlignmentThe proposed method mainly relies on spatial features in the hierarchical optimization, which is sensitive to initial alignment. Although the spatial dependency was alleviated by introducing mean curvature, incorporation of other spatial invariant features such as local geometry , known landmarks (e.g., sulcal curves) or spatial invariant descriptors (3D SIFT , 3D deep shape descriptors ) would offer robust particle optimization. Furthermore, in this work, we only aim at an intra-subject correspondence. In the future, the proposed method can be extended to inter-subject correspondence with reasonable initial alignment and proper spatial invariant features.4.5Potential ApplicationsThe proposed method established a more accurate correspondence with respect to boundary distances, DSC, parcellation-off ratio and self-intersection than baselines. From the well-established shape correspondence, our method provides a promising tool to analyze and assess shape quantifications after surgery, which suggests potential clinical applications in neurosurgical brain analysis. We showed the post-surgical cortical thickness changes using the correspondence established by the proposed method (Fig. 11). Since our shape correspondence is smooth and improved, high reliability is expected in capturing actual ROI-wise cortical thickness changes as well as other surface quantifications. In potential clinical applications, understanding of structural changes could lay the groundwork to prognosis and follow-up treatment on the basis of post-operative imaging findings. Furthermore, our methods may translate to other fields involving neurosurgical resection such as removal of intrinsic brain tumors. In the future, our method could be applied to assess the clinical outcome of treatment in detail.4.6LimitationsThe proposed method showed promising performance in the correspondence problem with temporal lobe resection. However, this work has several limitations. First, we mainly used spatial coordinate as the feature in the hierarchical optimization procedure. It is not fully robust to the spatial alignment by using spatial coordinate feature. In the future, we could try other spatial invariant features. Second, we aimed at an intra-subject correspondence in this work. In the following study, we may try to extend this method to an inter-subject correspondence. Also, as the proposed particles are independent, we could further accelerate the computing time. Furthermore, in this work, we only focused on temporal lobe resection. Hopefully, our method would be used in other correspondence problem with different surgeries and datasets in the future.
conclusion: 5.ConclusionIn this work, we proposed a particle method for cortical shape correspondence in temporal lobe resection. In our pipeline, we incorporate the neighborhood relationship and hierarchical optimization to avoid local minima. This strategy helps improve correspondence accuracy (Figs.5-6). We used the transferred parcellation (Figs.7-8), boundary HD (Fig.9), DSC (Table 1) and parcellation-off ratio (Table 2) to show that our method could outperform spherical methods which have distortion caused by spherical mapping in resection. By considering neighborhood information, our method could provide a smooth correspondence with no self-intersection and keep topology than point-wise matching methods which do not model neighborhood (Section 3.4.3). Furthermore, our method could be more reliable in capturing the actual cortical thickness changes after surgery, which provides a potential way of understanding accurate quantification of brain structural changes before and after surgical resection (Section 3.4.4).
figure_legends: The spherical parametrization with and w/o temporal lobe resection. Circled region shows the distortion in spherical methods after spherical parametrization. Due to the distortion, spherical methods may not fully address cortical correspondence problem in temporal lobe resection.

Example shape for post-surgical surface. It defines the number of particles and their distribution. The number of particles is 163842 (∣Ppre∣ = ∣Ppost∣ = 163842). They are ordered and located uniformly on the surface. Each of them has 5-6 neighborhood points, which is shown in the zoomed in figure. Initially, the locations of Ppre and Ppost are not matched.

Schematic overview of particle movement on the triangular mesh. (a)-(b) two cases show that particles move in the direction of g on the triangular mesh. u(m−1) indicates the initial location of a particle on the pre-surgical surface Ωpre at iteration m. In (a), particle u(m−1) is first moved to x′ which is on edge, and then to p3′ in the direction of g. In (b), particle u(m−1) is moved to x′ which is on a vertex. (c) Particle stops moving when the length of the trajectory is equal to α∥g∥. The complete particle movement procedure is summarized in Algorithm 1.

Particle locations during hierarchical optimization. (a) initial locations of particles Ppre (blue) on Ωpre. (b) particles Ppre (blue) on Ωpre are updated using Algorithm 1 to be matched to Ppost on Ωpost (red in (f) and (g)). (c) after the optimization, new neighborhood particles (orange) are added by an icosahedral subdivision scheme, in which they are placed at midpoints of edges formed by the original particles. Then, we repeat the optimization and subdivision procedure in (d-e). The hierarchical particle division repeats up to 7 levels of the icosahedral subdivision (∣Ppre∣ = ∣Ppost∣ = 163,842) for a dense shape correspondence.

Parcellation map of full optimization and hierarchical optimization on clinical data. The white circles represent regions which are mismatched after correspondence establishment. (a) the parcellation map on pre-surgical cortical surface. (b) the transferred parcellation map on post-surgical surface using full optimization without hierarchical update. (c) the transferred parcellation map on post-surgical surface using hierarchical optimization. After optimization, we expect the post-surgical parcellation is similar to pre-surgical one in (a). The circled regions are mismatched with those in (a), which means an unpromising correspondence. Compared with full optimization, the hierarchical optimization could lead to better correspondence while reducing a chance of trappings in local minima.

Intermediate transferred maps during the hierarchical optimization on clinical data. (a) pre-surgical surface with parcellation map. (b)-(h) post-surgical surfaces with the transferred parcellation maps in each level. After optimization, we expect the post-surgical parcellation is similar to pre-surgical one in (a). With the increase of levels, the correspondence becomes more accurate. In the highest level (level 7), the established correspondence shows the best performance in (h). The circled region with arrows shows the correspondence improvement in consecutive level. We observe improvement in circled region with the increase of levels.

The parcellation transfer in the simulated data. The white circles represent regions which are mismatched after correspondence establishment. After optimization, we expect the post-surgical parcellation is similar to pre-surgical one in (a), which means a better correspondence. The circled regions are mismatched with those in (a), which means an unpromising correspondence. (b-e) parcellation map with the spherical methods. They provide smooth correspondences (no self-intersection (S-I)). However, they are sensitive to spherical mapping distortion. Therefore, they could not achieve an accurate correspondence. (f-h) parcellation map with the point-wise matching methods. (f) and (g) offer good parcellation transfer in boundary alignment. However, their established correspondence could not avoid S-I which causes the small isolated area in (f-h). The self-intersection will be discussed in Section 3.4.3. Our method offers a smooth correspondence (no S-I) with small boundary alignment errors, which means our correspondence is more accurate and smooth.

The parcellation transfer in the clinical data. The white circles represent regions which are mismatched after correspondence establishment. After optimization, we expect the post-surgical parcellation is similar to pre-surgical one in (a), which means better a correspondence. The circled regions are mismatched with those in (a), which means an unpromising correspondence. We have similar observations with Fig. 7. The spherical methods in (b-e) show worse correspondences with more mismatched regions in parcellation. Point cloud methods show better correspondences with self-intersection which causes the small isolated area in (f-h).

ROI-wise boundary distances in the simulated data. The asterisk (*) in (a) indicates the proposed method outperforms the baseline methods after multi-comparison correction over ROIs by FDR at q = 0.05. Our method has no HD larger than 6mm, while other methods have 3-19 ROIs. This observation indicates that our method has accurate transferred parcellation and establish better correspondence.

Self-intersection of ICP, CPD and SW in the simulated and clinical data. The first row is the simulated data and the second row is the clinical data. The scattered pink shows the regions which have self-intersection (S-I). We only found S-I in point-wise matching methods. No scattered regions are found in our method and spherical methods.

ROI-wise cortical thickness difference between pre- and post-surgical surfaces in clinical data. The asterisk (*) indicates the proposed method has significant difference with the baseline methods. In some of ROIs, spherical methods have cortical changes larger than 0.6mm. In most of ROIs, most of the methods have thickness changes less than 0.4 mm. Combined with Fig. 12, the larger difference might be caused by the registration errors. Our method could be more reliable in capturing the actual cortical thickness changes after surgery.

The parcellation transfer of IstCg in the clinical data. In (b-e) and (h), the transferred parcellation is noticeably off. Point cloud methods (f-g) have small isolated clusters in their shape correspondence. Since our method offers better transferred parcellation, combined with Fig. 11, our method could be more reliable in capturing the actual cortical thickness changes after surgery.

Portion of false positives in the non-resected regions of the clinical data across 25 subjects with different threshold. Almost no normal region (0.3% out of a total of the non-resected surface area) is classified as resected after threshold t = 7. Therefore, we set t to 7 mm to estimate the resected region for all subjects in this work.
